LSO500 · Universal Design Liana Kadyrova · 159738228
Place Critique · Final Project

Woodbridge
Public Library

A critical assessment of accessibility, Universal Design principles, and the gap between perception and reality at a community institution.

Location 150 Woodbridge Ave, ON
Date April 13, 2026
Instructor Prof. Nancy La Monica
Scroll to explore
01

Introduction

The Woodbridge Public Library, located near Market Lane plaza and considered part of the historical centre of the village, was selected as the subject of this critique due to its high level of social interaction and public significance. Built in 1971, the library has undergone several renovations in response to changing social needs, with major updates in 2000 and 2014 focused on Universal Design (UD) principles.

Public libraries are commonly perceived as inclusive and welcoming environments. However, such assumptions often rely on generalized standards rather than detailed, user-centered evaluations. This report critically assesses the space by comparing publicly available information and user-generated reviews with direct personal observations — paying particular attention to the needs of individuals with mobility and sensory impairments.

Key frameworks consulted include the Ontario Human Rights Commission's definition of disability, Star Ford's essay on deep accessibility, and the Autistic Self Advocacy Network's notes on sensory and cognitive access needs.

Site at a Glance
Address 150 Woodbridge Ave, Woodbridge, ON L4L 2S7
Built 1971
Major Renovations 2000, 2014
Managed by Vaughan Public Libraries
Focus Universal Design & Community Access
02

Accessibility Issues: Wheelchair Users

Despite the positive framing in the online review, direct observation identified four significant barriers for wheelchair users.

🚪
No Push Button at Main Entrance

The automatic sliding doors rely solely on a motion sensor. There is no redundant push-button option in case of technical failure — a gap in inclusive entry design.

🪜
No Internal Ramps

Multiple building levels are connected by stairs, and some areas — including the television reading area — are not reachable via elevator, making them inaccessible to wheelchair users.

🚨
Inaccessible Emergency Exit

The rear emergency exit cannot be used by wheelchair users from either inside or outside the building, raising serious safety and evacuation concerns.

🅿️
Disconnected Accessible Parking

Accessible parking spots are located behind the building near the emergency exit, with no direct pathway to the main entrance — forcing users to travel around the perimeter.

03

Accessibility Issues: Visual Impairments

As a public institution, the library is expected to provide equal access to all users, including those with visual impairments. The analysis reveals significant gaps.

👣
No Tactile Floor Guidance

There is no tactile floor guidance system anywhere in the building to assist users with visual impairments in independent navigation.

🔤
Inconsistent Braille Signage

Braille is limited to washroom gender signs. There is no consistent Braille signage for other areas of the library, preventing independent wayfinding.

🎧
No Assistive Listening Resources

No audiobooks or headphone-equipped computer stations were found. Access to information in alternative formats is a core library function not met here.

04

Spatial Design & Wayfinding

The internal design does not align with the third principle of Universal Design — Simple and Intuitive Use. According to the Autistic Self Advocacy Network, clear and consistent visual communication is crucial for reducing confusion and improving the experience of users with cognitive or sensory differences.

Navigation within the library is difficult due to poorly designed signage. Labels indicating different book sections are small, visually worn, and some appear to be temporary paper signs attached with tape. The categorization system is also unclear — books are not consistently arranged by author or title, and no intuitive logic guides their placement. This creates challenges for all visitors, not only those with disabilities.

06

Review Analysis

A user review from the Access Now platform, written in 2023 by reviewer spsosa01, forms the basis of comparison for this site analysis. The review presents a broadly positive assessment of the library's accessibility.

"I would say Woodbridge Public Library is accessible. Not only do they have multiple ramps to go in and out of the facility, but they also have large elevators to go to the different levels of the building. They also have automatic opening doors and handicap parking as well as accessible washrooms on every floor."

— spsosa01, Access Now (2023)

The review highlights ramps, elevators, automatic doors, accessible parking, braille on washroom signage, and one staff member with sign-language skills. While these features align with common accessibility expectations, a personal on-site analysis revealed several inconsistencies between the review and actual conditions.

07

Site Documentation

Photos taken on-site during the accessibility assessment, April 2026.

08

Recommendations

Two levels of improvement are proposed. The first involves low-cost changes requiring no structural redesign. The second involves more substantial investment but addresses the most critical barriers.

Quick Win

Add tactile floor markings and Braille signage throughout the building to create a consistent, navigable environment for visually impaired users.

Quick Win

Install a redundant push-button door-opening mechanism at the main entrance to ensure access independence regardless of sensor functionality.

Quick Win

Redesign the book classification system with a clear, logical arrangement and replace worn or temporary signage with large, permanent, legible labels.

Quick Win

Provide audiobooks and headphone-equipped computer stations to ensure information is accessible in alternative formats for visually impaired visitors.

Structural

Install ramps or platform lifts to make all levels and reading areas accessible to wheelchair users — particularly the television reading area currently unreachable by elevator.

Structural

Redesign the accessible parking layout to include a direct, clearly marked pathway to the main entrance; ensure the emergency exit is usable by all users for safe evacuation.

09

Conclusion

The Woodbridge Public Library cannot be considered fully accessible despite having several basic accessibility features. While it meets some general standards, these elements are implemented inconsistently and do not form a cohesive system. A deeper analysis reveals significant gaps in both physical and sensory accessibility, limiting independent use for many visitors.

As Lesa Bradshaw emphasizes in her TEDx Talk, accessibility must go beyond compliance with general standards — designers must consider specific user experiences and avoid treating accessibility as a one-size-fits-all solution. To become truly inclusive, this space requires a comprehensive redesign that prioritizes user experience, intuitive navigation, and equal access for all individuals regardless of their abilities.

Overall Assessment
⚠️
"Partially accessible, but not fully inclusive. Significant gaps remain for wheelchair and visually impaired users."
10
